beats.prompt: Quick recall. From the last lesson, match each pool size to the BEST chance any look-then-leap strategy can achieve.

feedback.correct: Right — 1/2, 11/24, 13/30. These are the peak probabilities from Lesson 2, each found by scanning the success curve and picking the cutoff r* that maximises p_n(r).
hints: Each answer is the probability p_n(r*) at the optimal skip cutoff for that n.
For 3 candidates r*=2 gives p=1/2. For 4, r*=2 gives 11/24. For 5, r*=3 gives 13/30.

beats.prompt: A recruitment firm screens 1,000,000 applicants in random order. Decisions are irrevocable. Running the very best look-then-leap strategy, what is the highest chance of hiring the single best applicant?

basically 0%.note: Not so. Even with a million candidates the optimal strategy keeps a fighting chance — it does not collapse to zero. The answer is surprisingly large.

about 37%.note: Exactly right. The best possible win probability for 1,000,000 candidates is approximately 1/e ≈ 36.8% — barely distinguishable from the result for just 10 candidates.

about 50%.note: Too high. The best the strategy can ever do approaches 1/e ≈ 37% as n grows — not 50%. More candidates makes the problem harder, not easier.

beats.prompt: One constant to know before we go further.

interaction.title: Meet 1/e
interaction.body: e ≈ 2.718; 1/e ≈ 0.368 ≈ 37%. This one number is the limit both the skip fraction and the win chance settle on.

feedback.correct: Keep 1/e ≈ 0.368 in mind — it will show up in both the optimal cutoff fraction AND the best-case win probability.
hints: e is Euler's number, approximately 2.718.
1/e ≈ 0.368, which rounds to 37%.
Read it, then tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Watch both quantities settle. As n grows, the optimal skip fraction r*/n and the best-case win probability P(best) both drift toward the same limit. Can you see where they are heading?

feedback.correct: Both r*/n and P(best) converge to 1/e ≈ 0.368. At n=50 the optimal cutoff is already r*=19 — 38% of 50, almost exactly 37%.
hints: The skip fraction is r*/n; the win probability is p_n(r*). Both are plotted here.
As n grows, both curves drift toward a horizontal line.
That limit is 1/e ≈ 0.368 ≈ 37%.

beats.prompt: Explore the win-probability curve for 10 candidates. Drag the cutoff slider and watch the curve rise then fall — the peak marks the optimal r*.

feedback.correct: The peak sits at r*=4, giving p_10(4) = 3349/8400 ≈ 39.9%. That 40% is already dipping toward 37% — and it only gets closer as n grows.
hints: Sliding too far right means you might already have passed the best candidate during the look phase.
Sliding too far left means you accept someone before you have learned the field.
The sweet spot for n=10 is r*=4, giving about 39.9% — on its way to 37%.

beats.prompt: Pin down the exact numbers for n = 10.

feedback.correct: r* = 4 for n = 10. That is 4/10 = 40% — already close to 37%, and the ratio only tightens as n grows.
hints: The success curve for n=10 peaks at the smallest r giving the highest p_10(r).
That peak is at r=4; 4 out of 10 is 40%.
Answers: r* = 4, percent = 40.

beats.interviewNote: The exam punchline: both r*/n and P_n(r*) converge to 1/e as n → ∞. Prove it by maximising f(x) = x · ln(1/x) over x ∈ (0,1] — the continuous limit of the secretary-problem success formula. Setting f'(x) = ln(1/x) − 1 = 0 gives x = 1/e. The same constant governs both the optimal skip fraction and the optimal win chance, and it is entirely scale-free: whether n is 100 or 100 million, skip the first ~37% and your win chance is ~37%.
beats.prompt: One limit rules both quantities. Three lenses, one constant.

feedback.correct: 1/e governs both the optimal threshold and the optimal win probability. No matter how large the pool, the strategy is always the same: skip the first ~37%, then take the next record-setter.
hints: The skip fraction r*/n and the win probability p_n(r*) are two different quantities.
Yet both converge to the same constant 1/e ≈ 0.368 as n grows.
Three lenses: skip fraction → 1/e; win chance → 1/e; scale-free.

beats.prompt: State it in one number. As n grows without bound, both the optimal skip fraction and the best-case win probability approach 1/e. What is 1/e as a whole percent?

fields.label: As n → infinity, both fractions approach 1/e ≈ what whole percent?

feedback.correct: 37%. For n=100 the optimal cutoff is r*=38 (38%, almost exactly 37%); for 1,000,000 it is r*≈368,000 (exactly 36.8%). The ratio locks in at 1/e ≈ 37%.
hints: As a percentage: 0.3679 × 100 ≈ 36.79%, which rounds to 37%.
The answer is 37.

interaction.scenario: A firm screens \(n = 100\) candidates in random order, irrevocable decisions. Their analyst finds the optimal cutoff is \(r^* = 38\). (a) The look phase automatically rejects the first \(r^* - 1\) candidates — what percent of the 100 is that? (b) Approximately what percent chance does the look-then-leap rule give of landing the single best candidate from any large pool?

fields.label: Approx win percent for any large pool

feedback.correct: \[ r^* - 1 = 38 - 1 = 37 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{37}{100} = 37\% \] The look phase rejects exactly 37 of the 100 candidates — 37%. The win probability also converges to \(1/e \approx 37\%\). Both faces of the 37% rule are present: skip 37% to earn a ~37% shot at the best.
hints: The look phase rejects the first r*−1 candidates automatically. With r*=38, that is 37 candidates.
37 out of 100 is 37%. The win probability for large n also converges to 1/e ≈ 37%.
Both answers are 37.

feedback.correct: The 37% rule: skip the first 1/e ≈ 37% of candidates and your chance of landing the very best is also ~37% — regardless of pool size. Next: Why 37% Works — the calculus that pins 1/e as the exact limit.
hints: Both r*/n and P(best) converge to 1/e ≈ 0.368 ≈ 37%.
This limit is scale-free: same fraction whether n is 100 or 100 million.
Next lesson: the derivation that proves why 1/e is the exact answer.
